topic_title: Data types — string, integer, float, boolean

required_sections.detail: All four required sections present and above word minimums. Section 3 (LOs) ~90 w >= 50 w. Section 4 (Introduction) ~130 w >= 50 w. Section 5 (Core Concepts) ~1400 w >= 100 w. Section 10 (Key Takeaways) ~130 w >= 50 w. Unchanged from cycle 3.

citation_density.detail: All 5 primary resources cited at least once. Citation markers appear on the majority of standalone factual assertions in sections 5, 6, and 7; estimated rate ~73%, above the 60% threshold. Unchanged from cycle 3.

cycle_4_fix_confirmed.detail: The line `print(0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3)    # False` has been replaced with a comment: `# Note: 0.1 + 0.2 is NOT equal to 0.3 — the result is very slightly different`. The `==` operator no longer appears in any runnable code block. The float-precision point is made entirely through `print(0.1 + 0.2)` (which outputs `0.30000000000000004`) plus the explanatory comment — no comparison operator executed.

secondary_findings_soft.location: §7 — prose sentence: 'practitioners check whether a score is above a threshold (e.g., `similarity_score > 0.75`)'
secondary_findings_soft.detail: The `>` comparison operator appears as an inline code snippet inside a real-world patterns prose paragraph, not in a code block. This is illustrating a downstream professional pattern, not teaching the operator. Persists from cycle 3 as a soft finding; does not constitute a hard failure.

secondary_findings_soft.location: §8 — rule: 'Do not compare floats with `==`. Use `>=`, `<=`, or `round()` to test equality within a tolerance.'
secondary_findings_soft.detail: Comparison operator symbols appear in §8 Best Practices as names in a rule, not in active code. This is a mention-in-prose usage; it does not demonstrate or teach the operators. Persists from cycle 3 as a soft finding.

secondary_findings_soft.location: §9 — step 5: 'Create a boolean variable `distinction` that is `True` when the average is 70 or above'
secondary_findings_soft.detail: The exercise asks the learner to set `distinction = True` when average >= 70. A learner could also hard-code `distinction = True` without using a comparison operator. Persists from cycle 3 as a soft finding; recommend rewording to: 'Create a boolean variable `distinction` and assign it the value `True` (a distinction mark is 70 or above — you will automate this check in 11.5)'.

secondary_findings_soft.term: != in section heading and key-takeaway prose
secondary_findings_soft.location: §5.4 heading ('#### Why `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3`') and §10 bullet ('The result `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3` is not a bug...')
secondary_findings_soft.detail: `!=` appears as a mathematical notation embedded in prose headings/bullets to name the phenomenon, not in a runnable code block. The surrounding prose explains the concept without executing a comparison. This is a borderline mention-in-prose usage; does not constitute a hard failure.

sequence_drift.scan_result: No comparison operators (==, !=, >=, <=, <, >) appear in any runnable Python code block. All occurrences are in prose, headings, or comment lines.

length_sanity.detail: ~2950 words estimated. Comfortably within the 1500–4000 standard-profile corpus range. Unchanged from cycle 3.

topic_scope.detail: No scope creep. Both cycle-2 hard-fail terms ('implicit type conversion' bolded in §5.3, 'short-circuit evaluation' bolded in §5.5) confirmed absent. No new out-of-scope concepts introduced in cycle 4. Unchanged from cycle 3.

soft_findings.detail: Both terms introduced without a plain-English gloss at first use. The surrounding explanation (binary representation, 0.1 + 0.2 example) is strong, but the opening sentence uses these terms cold. 2 soft findings — at the threshold but below the >= 3 escalation threshold. Unchanged from cycles 2 and 3.

beginner_readability.detail: 2 soft findings; below the >= 3-finding escalation threshold. Overall beginner readability remains strong. Unchanged from cycles 2 and 3.

cycle_3_defect_fixed: sequence_drift: `print(0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3)` in §5.4 code block — confirmed removed; replaced with comment `# Note: 0.1 + 0.2 is NOT equal to 0.3 — the result is very slightly different`
soft_findings_persisting: sequence_drift soft: `similarity_score > 0.75` in §7 prose (inline, not code block)
sequence_drift soft: comparison operator symbols mentioned (not demonstrated) in §8 best-practices prose
sequence_drift soft: §9 exercise step 5 implies comparison operator use to complete — recommend rewording to allow hard-coded bool value
sequence_drift soft: `!=` used as notation in §5.4 section heading and §10 key-takeaway bullet (prose/heading, not runnable code)
beginner_readability: 'IEEE 754' and 'double-precision floating-point arithmetic' used without plain-English gloss in §5.4 — 2 soft findings, below escalation threshold

verdict: PASS (cycle 4). The sole remaining hard failure from cycle 3 is confirmed fixed: `print(0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3)` has been replaced with a comment line (`# Note: 0.1 + 0.2 is NOT equal to 0.3 — the result is very slightly different`). No comparison operators appear in any runnable Python code block in the corpus. All previous hard failures across cycles 1–3 are confirmed absent. Five soft findings persist (inline `>` in §7 prose, operator names in §8 prose, §9 exercise implying comparison use, `!=` as mathematical notation in headings/bullets, and the two undefined tech-terms in §5.4 opening) — none independently escalate to a hard fail. All required checks pass: required sections present, all 5 primary resources cited, no forward references in active code, word count ~2950 within standard-profile range, no scope creep, beginner readability strong. Corpus is approved to advance to artifact generation.
